name: Tungsten
name_origin: Name from Swedish 'tung sten' meaning 'heavy stone'. The chemical symbol W comes from 'wolfram', the German name for the mineral wolframite from which the element was first isolated in 1783 by the Elhuyar brothers.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Tungsten is moderately rare in Earth's crust at about 1.3 ppm. It is found principally in the minerals scheelite and wolframite. China dominates global production, supplying roughly 80% of world tungsten, with additional output from Vietnam, Russia, and Bolivia.
states: trace in granitic and skarn deposits

compounds.name: Tungsten carbide
compounds.summary: An extremely hard ceramic used in cutting tools, mining bits, armor-piercing ammunition, and jewelry.

compounds.name: Tungsten trioxide
compounds.summary: A yellow oxide used as a precursor to tungsten metal, in electrochromic smart windows, and as a yellow pigment.

compounds.name: Tungsten hexafluoride
compounds.summary: A dense colorless gas used in semiconductor manufacturing for chemical vapor deposition of tungsten contact plugs.

compounds.name: Sodium tungstate
compounds.summary: A water-soluble salt used as a fireproofing agent for textiles and as a precursor in tungsten chemistry.

compounds.name: Tungsten disulfide
compounds.summary: A layered semiconductor used as a dry lubricant and as a 2D material for next-generation electronics.

uses: Filaments in incandescent lamps and electron-emission cathodes
Hardmetal cutting tools and drill bits as tungsten carbide
Heavy-metal alloys for kinetic-energy penetrators and radiation shielding
Counterweights and ballast in aerospace, motorsport, and watches due to extreme density
X-ray tube targets and rotating anodes
High-temperature furnace heating elements and rocket nozzle inserts
reactivity_summary: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any element (3695 K) and one of the highest tensile strengths. It is corrosion resistant and only mildly attacked by most mineral acids; aqua regia and a mix of HF and HNO3 dissolve it. It oxidizes slowly in air above ~400 C to form WO3 and reacts with halogens, carbon, and boron at elevated temperature. Pure tungsten is brittle when impure but ductile when very pure.
isotopes_summary: Tungsten has five naturally occurring isotopes: 180W, 182W, 183W, 184W, and 186W. All are believed to be alpha-radioactive but with extraordinarily long half-lives (>10^18 years), so tungsten is effectively stable. 184W (~30.6%) and 186W (~28.4%) are the most abundant. Many synthetic isotopes are used in nuclear medicine and as tracers.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
